column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Cast Iron Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/metal/ferrous/cast-iron-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for Cast Iron oxide removal without substrate damage
Near-infrared wavelength optimized for Cast Iron absorption and rust removal
Beam spot diameter for effective cleaning resolution
Optimal repetition rate for continuous cleaning coverage
Fluence threshold for Cast Iron oxide removal
Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient oxide layer ablation
Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage
Number of cleaning passes for complete oxide removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform surface treatment

Typical range for industrial cleaning of cast iron to remove rust or contaminants without substrate damage; starting at 200W balances efficiency and safety. Max limited to avoid melting (cast iron melting point ~1150-1300°C). Source: IPG Photonics laser cleaning systems specs for metal surfaces; academic review in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2018).

Effective range for 1064 nm cleaning on cast iron; slower for heavy contamination (e.g., thick rust) to ensure ablation, faster for light oils. Recommended 2000 mm/s for optimal throughput with minimal heat input, considering cast iron's high thermal conductivity (~50 W/m·K). Source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals; case studies in Journal of Cleaner Production (2020).

Nanosecond regime dominant for industrial cleaning of cast iron to achieve plasma-induced ablation with low heat-affected zone (HAZ); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precise but costlier applications. 50 ns recommended to minimize thermal diffusion in cast iron (reflectivity ~40% at 1064 nm). Source: Laser physics research in Applied Surface Science (2019); avoids fs due to overkill for contamination removal.

Standard range for pulsed fiber lasers in metal cleaning; 100 kHz balances cleaning speed and heat accumulation on cast iron, where high rates (>150 kHz) risk subsurface heating despite good conductivity. Trade-off: higher freq accelerates process but increases cumulative fluence. Source: Academic literature in Lasers in Manufacturing and Materials Processing (2021).

Fluence range for safe removal of contaminants like rust or paint on cast iron; damage threshold ~2-4 J/cm² to avoid pitting or melting. 1.5 J/cm² recommended for effective cleaning below ablation threshold of cast iron (~3 J/cm² at 1064 nm). Conservative due to cast iron's variable surface roughness. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2017); SPIE proceedings on laser cleaning.

Beam diameter range for industrial scanners; smaller for fine details (e.g., crevices in castings), larger for broad areas. 200 μm recommended for uniform coverage on rough cast iron surfaces without excessive overlap heating. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems; process optimization in Surface and Coatings Technology (2022).

Typically 1-3 passes for light to moderate contamination on cast iron; multi-pass for layered buildup (e.g., old paint). 2 passes recommended for complete removal while minimizing total energy input and HAZ. Single pass viable for thin rust. Source: Industry practice from case studies in International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (2019); manufacturer guidelines.

Standard range for scan line overlap to ensure uniform fluence distribution; 60% recommended for cast iron to achieve even cleaning on irregular surfaces without gaps or over-ablation. Higher (70-80%) for heavy contamination. Source: Process optimization studies in Optics and Laser Technology (2020); based on Gaussian beam profiles in cleaning simulations.
